food_subgroup: Whole red snapper (Lutjanus spp.) steamed over water with light soy sauce, dark soy sauce, sesame oil, shredded ginger, spring onion, coriander, and sizzling hot oil poured over — Chinese zi char restaurant preparation

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for steamed red snapper Chinese style. FOODON_03301116 (fish food product, steamed) is used as the closest defensible parent concept. A more specific term for Chinese-style steamed snapper with soy sauce does not exist in FoodOn as of April 2026.
ontology.foodex2_description: Red snapper (Lutjanus spp.) steamed Chinese style with light soy sauce, dark soy sauce, sesame oil, ginger, spring onion, coriander, and sizzling hot oil finish — composite dish (fish 250g + sauce 130g per 380g serving). FoodEx2 base term A0CJW (Snapper) used as closest parent; no exact facet combination for Chinese-style steamed preparation with soy sauce exists in FoodEx2 matrix as of April 2026.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW (Snapper) is the closest defensible base term. No exact FoodEx2 facet combination for Chinese-style steamed red snapper composite dish with soy sauce condiment exists. Code assigned at species level only; preparation and sauce facets are not available in the current FoodEx2 catalogue for this specific dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code applicable for this prepared dish. Not assigned to avoid fabrication.

serving.description: 1 serving steamed red snapper (~380g: whole fish 250g + sauce 130g composite)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Chinese-style steamed red snapper consists of approximately 380g total — a whole fish of about 250g edible weight steamed over water, finished with approximately 130g of sauce comprising light soy sauce, dark soy sauce, sesame oil, shredded ginger, spring onion, coriander, and sizzling hot oil poured over at the table.

nutrient_source.title: Red Snapper — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F03010013
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo Food Code F03010013 provides the base nutrient profile for red snapper. Values represent the composite dish (steamed fish + soy-based sauce) at 380g per serving. Sodium value (380mg/100g) reflects the composite dish including soy sauce contribution. USDA FoodData Central (NDB 15159, Red snapper, mixed species, cooked, dry heat) used as secondary cross-reference for micronutrient validation. Per-100g values are consistent with HPB source.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index — University of Sydney GI Database; not applicable for negligible-carbohydrate fish dish
gi_source.source_match_notes: Steamed red snapper Chinese style contains only 0.5g carbohydrate per 100g (approximately 1.9g per 380g serving). Per University of Sydney GI Database methodology, foods with negligible carbohydrate content are not assigned a glycemic index value as there is insufficient carbohydrate to produce a measurable glycemic response. glycemic_index is set to 0 as a sentinel numeric value indicating not applicable (gi_category: not_applicable); glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0. No GI testing is warranted or available for this food type.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from soy sauce — approximately 1,444mg sodium per 380g serving
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request sauce on the side or reduce sauce volume, which can lower sodium intake by approximately 800–900mg per serving; the fish itself provides beneficial potassium (420mg/100g) and omega-3 fatty acids that offer modest antihypertensive support.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (0.5g/100g; ~1.9g per serving) with very high protein (20g/100g; ~76g per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as its negligible carbohydrate content produces no meaningful postprandial glucose response, and its very high protein content (76g per serving) supports satiety and glycaemic stability; vitamin D (3.5mcg/100g) may additionally support insulin sensitivity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High-quality lean protein, low saturated fat (0.4g/100g), omega-3 fatty acids (~0.75g DHA+EPA per serving), and selenium (38mcg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Steamed red snapper is a cardioprotective food choice due to its very low saturated fat, absence of trans fat, and meaningful omega-3 content (~0.75g DHA+EPA per 380g serving); sodium from soy sauce should be moderated by requesting sauce on the side for patients with concurrent hypertension or heart failure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids, selenium antioxidant activity, and ginger-derived gingerols
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The modest omega-3 content (~0.75g DHA+EPA per serving) contributes to anti-inflammatory fatty acid balance, selenium (38mcg/100g, approximately 69% of daily AI) supports glutathione peroxidase antioxidant activity, and ginger in the aromatics provides additional anti-inflammatory gingerols.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Red Snapper Steamed Chinese Style (清蒸红鱼 / Ikan Merah Kukus Gaya Cina) is a classic Singaporean and Malaysian Chinese dish served at zi char restaurants and Chinese seafood restaurants. A whole red snapper (Lutjanus spp., typically Lutjanus sebae or Lutjanus erythrorhynchus) weighing approximately 250g edible weight is steamed over boiling water until just cooked, then finished with approximately 130g of sauce comprising light soy sauce, dark soy sauce, sesame oil, shredded ginger, spring onion, and coriander. The signature technique involves pouring 2–3 tablespoons of sizzling hot oil over the fish and aromatics at the table, creating a dramatic sizzle that blooms the ginger and spring onion fragrance. The flesh is tender, moist, and flaky with a clean, delicate flavour. Per 380g serving: approximately 372 kcal, 76g protein (very high), 6.8g fat, 1.9g carbohydrate, and approximately 1,444mg sodium (predominantly from soy sauce). Nutritionally, this dish is an excellent source of lean complete protein, selenium (38mcg/100g, ~69% daily AI), vitamin D (3.5mcg/100g), vitamin B12 (2.2mcg/100g), and omega-3 fatty acids (~0.75g DHA+EPA per serving). It is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes (negligible carbohydrate), cardiovascular health (low saturated fat, omega-3), and rheumatoid arthritis (anti-inflammatory profile). Sodium is the primary clinical concern for hypertension patients; requesting sauce on the side reduces sodium by approximately 800–900mg. Halal and dairy-free. A staple of CNY reunion dinners and Chinese family celebrations.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo Database (Food Code F03010013), an approved primary regional source for Singapore dishes. Values cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central NDB 15159 (red snapper, mixed species, cooked, dry heat) for micronutrient plausibility. Composite dish sodium value (380mg/100g) is consistent with a fish-plus-soy-sauce preparation. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent. Ontology anchored to FoodOn FOODON_03301116 (closest parent: fish food product steamed) and FoodEx2 A0CJW (Snapper, closest parent at species level).
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite steamed red snapper dish (fish + soy sauce) is not separately reported in HPB Foodo F03010013 or USDA FoodData Central NDB 15159 as of April 2026; estimated total omega-3 (~0.75g DHA+EPA per 380g serving) is cited in llm_training context based on USDA values for cooked red snapper. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish (fish + sauce) is not reported in HPB Foodo F03010013. trans_fat_g is confirmed 0.0 per HPB source. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed 0.0 per HPB source (fish has no dietary fibre; sauce aromatics contribute negligible fibre at composite level). Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.5g/100g); glycemic_index is set to numeric 0 as a sentinel value with gi_category 'not_applicable'; GL per serving is effectively 0.0. Sodium per serving (~1,444mg) is calculated as 380mg/100g × 380g serving; this is dominated by light soy sauce contribution. Sauce-on-side modification reduces sodium by approximately 800–900mg per serving. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03301116 used as closest parent (fish food product, steamed); FoodEx2 A0CJW (Snapper) used as closest parent at species level — no exact code for Chinese-style steamed preparation with soy sauce exists in either catalogue as of April 2026. Source: HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F03010013, accessed 2026-04-13.
